response:
## Decision: Run the full pre-registered grid (Option A) — do not pilot the expensive arm with early stop.

**Choice.** Execute all 4 arms (Haiku-solo, Sonnet-solo, Opus-solo, Tiered) × 8 tasks × 3 reps as pre-registered at ISSUE-41.md:33-43, analyzed per the decision rule at :51. Reject piloting Tiered (or Opus-solo) alone with an early-stop-if-decidable rule.

**Why Option B fails on the substance, not just process:**
- The pre-screen at :42 — "1-rep Haiku-solo vs Opus-solo pass; drop saturated tasks" — is not optional scaffolding, it's the mechanism the pre-reg itself names (:54) as what makes the test informative at all: "without a Haiku<Opus gap the test is uninformative." That pre-screen requires running the solo endpoints *before* the 8 tasks are even finalized, so "pilot the expensive arm first" is temporally incoherent — you can't select valid tasks without first touching Haiku-solo/Opus-solo.
- The decision rule (:51) is relative — delta and token-ratio are computed *against Sonnet-solo* — so a single expensive-arm pilot can't produce a decidable answer on its own; at minimum it needs a contemporaneous Sonnet-solo run in the pair, and per the premortem, a back-filled/non-contemporaneous baseline won't hold up to later reproduction.
- Sequential stopping is explicitly licensed by the pre-reg only in one direction — "add reps/tasks... stop when unambiguous" (:49) — as an escalation rule, not a de-escalation/early-exit rule. Stopping the whole grid early after peeking at the priciest arm is the "fishing for a favorable cut" the pre-registration (:25) exists to block, compounded by the already-conceded grader-independence residual (comment 3, owner call #1).

**Load-bearing assumption (the one thing that reverses this call if false):** that the Haiku<Opus gradient pre-screen at :42/:54 is validity-necessary — i.e., that a task set without a confirmed solo gradient genuinely produces an uninformative Tiered-vs-Sonnet comparison, rather than being a nice-to-have sanity check that could safely be inferred or skipped. If that's false — if the gradient could be reliably known some other way (e.g., from prior task metadata) without running fresh Haiku-solo/Opus-solo passes on these 8 tasks — then the "full grid" collapses to just Sonnet-solo + Tiered, and running all four arms in full becomes over-spend relative to what the decision rule actually consumes.

**Failure class accepted:** over-spend. Running Haiku-solo and Opus-solo as full 3-rep arms (not just the 1-rep pre-screen) costs real tokens/invocations (~250-300 per comment 3, :71) that the primary go/no-go arithmetic doesn't strictly need — we're buying validity (a confirmed gradient, a contemporaneous baseline, and the secondary Opus-quality report at :51) at that price, on purpose, because the pre-reg itself treats gradient confirmation as load-bearing rather than optional.

premortem.response:
Read ISSUE-41.md. Here's the premortem on the pilot-most-expensive-arm-and-early-stop design (no decision made, none implied):

**1. Comparator loss breaks the decision rule itself.** The pre-registered rule is relative, not absolute: adopt Tiered only if delta *vs Sonnet-solo* is ≥ -0.05 **and** tokens ≤ 0.7x *Sonnet-solo* (lines 31, 51), and the "real prize" question is explicitly phrased against *Opus-solo* quality (line 51). If piloting runs only the single most expensive arm first and calls the gate "already decidable" off that arm's numbers, there is no contemporaneous same-task-set baseline arm to compute the delta or the 0.7x ratio against — it has to be assumed or back-filled from a different run. Six months out, when someone tries to defend or reproduce the shipped call, the back-filled baseline (different session, possible model drift, different day's variance) doesn't match what the pilot implicitly assumed, and the "already decidable" verdict no longer holds against a real measurement.

**2. The gradient pre-screen is exactly the thing early-stopping skips.** Line 54 names the threat directly: "without a Haiku<Opus gap the test is uninformative; the pre-screen enforces it," and the pre-screen (line 42) requires running both solo endpoints first to pick the 8 tasks. A pilot-the-expensive-arm-first design is structured to *avoid* running the cheap/solo arms until forced to — which means the stop-early call gets made on tasks whose gradient was never confirmed by the pre-screen the plan requires. If those tasks later turn out saturated (all arms converge) once the skipped solo arms finally run, the early "unambiguous" result was unambiguous only because it was measuring a task set the pre-registration says produces no signal.

**3. Grader-independence residual compounds with optional stopping.** Comment 3's owner call #1 already concedes "grader independence is unsolvable with 3 tiers... accept the Sonnet-grades-Sonnet residual." Whichever arm counts as "most expensive" and gets piloted first (Opus-solo or Tiered, both Sonnet-adjacent in the grading chain), stopping the moment its blind-graded pass rate clears the bar means the single most decision-determining data point is the one carrying the weakest-known grader independence. The test plan already licenses sequential peeking "when unambiguous" (line 49) at the rep/task level; stretching that same license to stop the whole arm-grid early stacks a known measurement bias (Sonnet grading Sonnet-adjacent output) on top of a known statistical one (repeated looks, no pre-specified stopping correction). A result that clears the bar under both effects simultaneously is precisely the one least likely to survive a later re-run with a genuinely independent grader.

assumption.response:
Two realistic options for this call, per ISSUE-41.md:

**Option A — Run the full pre-registered grid** (4 arms × 8 tasks × 3 reps, ISSUE-41.md:33-43, then analyze per the decision rule at :51).
Load-bearing dependency: that Haiku-solo and Opus-solo are *required* outputs, not optional context. The decision rule itself (:51) only needs Tiered vs Sonnet-solo; the Opus comparison is explicitly secondary ("Also report whether Tiered reaches Opus-solo quality," :51). **If Haiku-solo/Opus-solo are optional context rather than decision-critical, Option A flips** — the "full grid" requirement collapses to a Tiered/Sonnet-solo pair, and running all four arms first is over-spend relative to what the gate needs.

**Option B — Pilot the most expensive arm first, stop early if the gate is already decidable.**
Load-bearing dependency: that an early stop after seeing results doesn't count as the "fishing for a favorable cut" the pre-registration exists to prevent (:25). The pre-reg only specifies a one-directional **sequential escalation** — "add reps/tasks... stop when unambiguous" (:49) — with no pre-specified de-escalation or early-stop-on-success rule. **If stopping early after peeking at the expensive arm's result is itself the fishing behavior the pre-reg was written to block, Option B flips** — it's not a lean variant of the registered design but a protocol deviation, reverting to Option A (or requiring a new pre-registered interim-analysis rule before any pilot can be run).

Secondary note: Option B is also underspecified on which arm is "most expensive" (Opus-solo is priciest per-token; Tiered is priciest per-invocation, :71) and that arm alone can't compute the decision rule's delta (:51) without the Sonnet-solo baseline — so any real B is at minimum a *pair* pilot (expensive arm + Sonnet-solo), not a true single-arm pilot.
